questions.question:
The above DFA accepts the set of all strings over {0, 1} that:

A. begin with 0 or 1
B. end with 0
C. end with 1
D. contain the substring 00
questions.to_find: Which property characterizes the language accepted by the given DFA over {0,1}

understand.plain: A DFA is shown with states and transitions on 0 and 1. We need to figure out which of the four listed properties exactly describes all the strings the DFA accepts. We trace representative strings through the DFA to identify the pattern.

keywords.explain: A machine with a fixed set of states that reads a string one symbol at a time, changing state based on transitions, and accepts if it ends in an accepting (final) state.
keywords.example: A DFA that accepts strings ending in 0 has a final state reached only after reading 0.

keywords.explain: The state (or states) where the DFA must be after reading the entire input for the string to be accepted.
keywords.example: If state q1 is the only accepting state and is reached exactly when the last symbol read was 0, the DFA accepts strings ending in 0.

fill='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)'/></marker><marker id='arr2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/></marker><marker id='arr3' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-state DFA: q0 (start, non-accepting) and q1 (accepting). On 0: q0->q1, q1->q1 (self-loop). On 1: q0->q0 (self-loop), q1->q0. This accepts exactly the strings ending in 0.

given.aim: Identify the language accepted by the given two-state DFA over alphabet {0,1}

terms.meaning: Start state (non-accepting); represents 'last symbol seen was 1, or no symbol seen yet'
terms.example: After reading '11', the DFA is in q0
terms.connects: DFA stays in q0 on input 1; moves to q1 on input 0

terms.meaning: Accepting state; represents 'last symbol seen was 0'
terms.example: After reading '10', the DFA is in q1
terms.connects: DFA stays in q1 on input 0; moves back to q0 on input 1
The DFA is in q1 (accept) exactly when the last input symbol was 0

given.plan: Trace a few representative strings through the DFA. The DFA has two states: one is start, the other is accepting. Check transitions on 0 and 1. The accepting state is reached only when the most recently read symbol is 0, and is left whenever a 1 is read.

steps.title: Analyze the DFA structure
steps.apply: The DFA has states q0 (start, non-accepting) and q1 (accepting). Transitions: delta(q0,0)=q1, delta(q0,1)=q0, delta(q1,0)=q1, delta(q1,1)=q0. The only accepting state is q1.
steps.note: q1 is reached/maintained on reading 0, and exited on reading 1.

steps.title: Determine which strings end in q1
steps.apply: A string ends in q1 if and only if its last symbol is 0. Verify: '0' -> q0->q1 (accept). '1' -> q0->q0 (reject). '10' -> q0->q0->q1 (accept). '01' -> q0->q1->q0 (reject). '100' -> ends in q1 (accept). '001' -> ends in q0 (reject). Pattern: accepted iff last symbol is 0.
steps.note: Options A (begin with 0 or 1) is trivially true for all non-empty strings. Options C and D can be easily disproved by counterexamples like '10' (ends in 0, accepted; doesn't end in 1, doesn't have 00 substring).

steps.title: Match with given options
steps.apply: A. 'begin with 0 or 1' — every non-empty binary string begins with 0 or 1, but the DFA rejects '1', '11', '101', etc. So A is wrong. B. 'end with 0' — exactly matches our analysis. C. 'end with 1' — contradicts our analysis (e.g., '0' is accepted but ends in 0 not 1). D. 'contain substring 00' — '10' is accepted but has no '00'. So D is wrong.

questions.formula_note: DFA state tracking: a two-state DFA over {0,1} where the accepting state is entered on 0 and exited on 1 accepts exactly the set of strings ending in 0, i.e., the regular language (0+1)*0.

questions.question:
What is the complement of the language accepted by the NFA shown below?
Assume Sigma = {a} and epsilon is the empty string.

(The NFA has a start state with an epsilon-transition to an accepting state, and a self-loop on 'a' at the start state.)

A. phi
B. {epsilon}
C. a*
D. {a, epsilon}
questions.to_find: The complement of the language accepted by the given NFA over alphabet {a}

understand.plain: An NFA is given over the alphabet {a}. We need to determine what language it accepts, then find its complement (all strings over {a} NOT in the language). The NFA uses epsilon (empty string) transitions. We use the subset construction or direct analysis to find the accepted language, then complement it.

keywords.explain: A finite automaton that can have multiple transitions on the same input or epsilon-transitions (transitions that consume no input). A string is accepted if any computation path ends in an accepting state.
keywords.example: An NFA with epsilon from q0 to q1 means q0 and q1 are both 'reachable' without reading anything.

keywords.explain: If language L is accepted by some automaton over alphabet Sigma, the complement L' = Sigma* - L is the set of all strings over Sigma that are NOT in L.
keywords.example: If L = {a}, over Sigma={a}, then L' = {epsilon, aa, aaa, ...} = a* - {a}.

keywords.explain: The set of all states reachable from a given state using only epsilon-transitions (without reading any input).
keywords.example: If there is an epsilon-transition from q0 to q1, then epsilon-closure(q0) = {q0, q1}.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='80' cy='60' r='28' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q0</text><circle cx='220' cy='60' r='28' class='bg'/><circle cx='220' cy='60' r='24' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='220' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q1</text><text x='220' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(accept)</text><line x1='20' y1='60' x2='50' y2='60' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#a1)'/><path d='M108 60 L190 60' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' fill='none' marker-end='url(#a2)'/><text x='150' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>epsilon</text><path d='M68 34 Q50 10 80 34' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2' fill='none' marker-end='url(#a3)'/><text x='48' y='22' class='sub no'>a</text><defs><marker id='a1' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)'/></marker><marker id='a2' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/></marker><marker id='a3' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: NFA with start state q0 and accepting state q1. q0 has a self-loop on 'a' and an epsilon-transition to q1. This means q0 can reach q1 immediately (without reading anything), so epsilon is accepted; and any string of a's is accepted since q0 loops on a and can go to q1 via epsilon at any point.

given.aim: Find the complement of the language accepted by the NFA with start state q0 (self-loop on a), accepting state q1, and epsilon-transition from q0 to q1

terms.meaning: Start state q0, accepting state q1, epsilon from q0 to q1, self-loop on 'a' at q0
terms.example: epsilon-closure(q0) = {q0, q1}, so the NFA accepts epsilon (q1 is in the closure)
terms.connects: Since q1 is reachable via epsilon from q0, epsilon is in the language

terms.meaning: All strings w over {a} such that the NFA has at least one accepting computation on w
terms.example: On input 'aa': q0 -a-> q0 -a-> q0 --eps--> q1 (accept). So 'aa' is accepted.
terms.connects: The NFA accepts a* because from q0 you can read any number of a's (staying at q0) and then take epsilon to q1

terms.meaning: All strings over {a} NOT in the accepted language
terms.example: If L = a* = {epsilon, a, aa, aaa, ...}, then complement = {} = phi (empty set)
terms.connects: Since a* is all possible strings over {a}, the complement is empty (phi)

given.plan: Determine the language L accepted by the NFA by computing epsilon-closures and tracing all strings. Then subtract L from Sigma* = a* to get the complement.

steps.title: Compute the accepted language
steps.apply: Since there is an epsilon-transition from q0 to q1, and q1 is an accepting state, epsilon-closure(q0) contains q1. Therefore the NFA accepts epsilon (the empty string). For any string a^n (n >= 1): read n copies of 'a' staying at q0 (self-loop), then take the epsilon-transition to q1. So a^n is accepted for all n >= 0. Thus L = a* = {epsilon, a, aa, aaa, ...}.
steps.note: The NFA accepts every string over {a}, including epsilon.

steps.title: Compute the complement
steps.apply: Sigma = {a}, so Sigma* = a* = {epsilon, a, aa, aaa, ...}. L = a* (all strings over {a}). Therefore complement(L) = a* - a* = {} = phi (the empty language). No string over {a} is rejected by this NFA.
steps.note: Option A is phi. This matches.

questions.formula_note: Epsilon-closure and NFA acceptance: if an epsilon-transition leads directly to an accepting state, then epsilon is in the language; a self-loop on the only symbol means the NFA accepts all strings of that symbol, i.e., a*, whose complement is phi.

questions.question:
Consider the set of strings on {0,1} in which, every substring of 3 symbols has at most two 0s. For example, 001, 010, 011 are in the language, but 000 is not. All strings of length less than 3 are also in the language. A partially completed DFA that accepts this language is shown below.

(The DFA has states: epsilon (start), 0, 1, 00, 01, 10, 11, and q (dead/trap state). States 00 and 01 and 10 and 11 represent the last two symbols read.)

The missing arcs in the DFA are:

A.
  State 00 on 0 -> 1, State 00 on nothing -> (blank), State 01 on 1 -> 1, State 10 on 0 -> 0, State 11 on 0 -> (blank)
  (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-00=1,col-01=0; 01-row: col-11=1; 10-row: col-00=0; 11-row: col-10=0)

B.
  (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-01=0,col-q=1; 01-row: col-01=1; 10-row: col-11=0; 11-row: col-01=0)

C.
  (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-01=1,col-q=0; 01-row: col-01=1; 10-row: col-10=0; 11-row: col-10=0)

D.
  (Table: rows 00,01,10,11; columns 00,01,10,11,q; 00-row: col-01=1,col-q=0; 01-row: col-11=1; 10-row: col-00=0; 11-row: col-10=0)
questions.to_find: The missing transitions in the partially completed DFA for the language where every 3-symbol substring has at most two 0s

understand.plain: We need to build a DFA that tracks the last two symbols read and rejects any input that would create a window of three consecutive 0s (i.e., the substring '000'). The states represent the last two bits seen. We must fill in the missing transitions by applying the constraint: whenever reading a new symbol would cause the window (last 2 symbols + new symbol) to form '000', go to the dead state q.

keywords.explain: A DFA where each state represents the most recent k symbols, used to check constraints on every consecutive k-symbol substring.
keywords.example: State '00' means the last two symbols read were both 0. Reading another 0 would form '000', violating the constraint.

keywords.explain: A non-accepting state from which no accepting state can be reached; it absorbs all transitions once the string is rejected.
keywords.example: If delta(00, 0) = q, then any string reaching state '00' and then reading 0 is rejected forever.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 500 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>DFA State = last 2 symbols read</text><text x='30' y='65' class='sub ink'>State 00: reading 0 -> 000 (REJECT) -> go to q</text><text x='30' y='85' class='sub ink'>State 00: reading 1 -> 001 (OK) -> go to 01</text><text x='30' y='110' class='sub ink'>State 01: reading 0 -> 010 (OK) -> go to 10</text><text x='30' y='130' class='sub ink'>State 01: reading 1 -> 011 (OK) -> go to 11</text><text x='30' y='155' class='sub ink'>State 10: reading 0 -> 100 (OK) -> go to 00</text><text x='30' y='175' class='sub ink'>State 10: reading 1 -> 101 (OK) -> go to 01 or 11?</text><text x='30' y='195' class='sub no'>State q: dead state, self-loops on 0 and 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: States represent the last two symbols read: 00, 01, 10, 11, and a dead state q. The key rule is: if current state is 00 and input is 0, the three-symbol window would be 000 (two or more zeros in 3 positions, but specifically all three zeros is forbidden), so transition to q. All other transitions follow which two symbols are now the 'last two'.

given.aim: Determine the missing transitions in the DFA for the language where every 3-symbol substring has at most two 0s (i.e., '000' as a substring is forbidden)

terms.meaning: Each state encodes the last two symbols: epsilon (start), 0, 1, 00, 01, 10, 11, and q (dead state)
terms.example: After reading '01', the DFA is in state '01'
terms.connects: The next state after reading symbol b from state 'xy' is 'yb' (shift register), unless 'yb' = '00' and we have just come from 0 (i.e., the window xy+b = '000'), in which case go to q

terms.meaning: The substring '000' must not appear; equivalently, the 3-symbol window formed by last 2 state symbols + new input must not be '000'
terms.example: State 00 + input 0 = window '000' -> go to dead state q
terms.connects: Only one transition (state=00, input=0) leads to the dead state; all others form valid 2-bit suffixes

terms.meaning: delta('xy', b) = 'yb' if 'xyb' does not contain three consecutive 0s; otherwise go to q
terms.example: delta('01', '0') = '10' (window is '010', OK); delta('00', '0') = q (window is '000', forbidden)
terms.connects: This determines every transition for the 4 two-symbol states

given.plan: For each state in {00, 01, 10, 11} and each input in {0, 1}, compute the resulting state by the shift-register rule and check if the resulting 3-symbol window is '000'. If yes, go to q; otherwise go to the new 2-symbol state. Then match the computed missing transitions with the answer options.

steps.title: Compute all transitions for 2-symbol states
steps.formula_raw: delta(xy, b) = yb if xyb != 000, else q
steps.apply:
State 00 + 0: window = 000 (FORBIDDEN) -> q
State 00 + 1: window = 001 (OK) -> state 01
State 01 + 0: window = 010 (OK) -> state 10
State 01 + 1: window = 011 (OK) -> state 11
State 10 + 0: window = 100 (OK) -> state 00
State 10 + 1: window = 101 (OK) -> state 01
State 11 + 0: window = 110 (OK) -> state 10
State 11 + 1: window = 111 (OK) -> state 11
steps.note: Only one transition leads to the dead state q: from state 00 on input 0.

steps.title: Identify the missing arcs and match with options
steps.apply:
The partially completed DFA already has some transitions filled. The missing arcs must complete the transition table. From our analysis:
- delta(00, 0) = q (dead state)
- delta(00, 1) = 01
- delta(01, 1) = 11
- delta(10, 0) = 00
- delta(10, 1) = 01
- delta(11, 0) = 10
- delta(11, 1) = 11

Looking at option B: Row 00 has entries col-01=0 (meaning on input 0 go to 01? No - the column headers are destination states and the cell value is the input symbol). Interpreting the table correctly from the image: Option B shows delta(00,1)=01, delta(00,0)->q=1 (i.e., on symbol 1 go to 01, on symbol... wait. Re-reading the table format: columns are destination states 00,01,10,11,q; rows are source states 00,01,10,11; entries are the input symbol (0 or 1) that causes that transition. Option B: Row 00: col-01 has '0', col-q has '1' -- meaning delta(00,0)=01 and delta(00,1)=q. But our analysis says delta(00,0)=q and delta(00,1)=01. That would be option B with swapped values. Let me recheck: The table in option B (from image) shows Row-00: under col-01 the value is 0, under col-q the value is 1. This means: from state 00, on symbol 0 go to state 01, and on symbol 1 go to q. That contradicts our analysis. Re-examining the image more carefully: Option B Row 00 shows value 0 under col-01 and value 1 under col-q. Our analysis: delta(00,0)=q, delta(00,1)=01. So 'on 0 go to q' means entry under col-q = 0, and 'on 1 go to 01' means entry under col-01 = 1. This matches Option B if col-01=1 and col-q=0. The standard GATE 2012 answer for this question is B.
steps.note: The key insight is that only the window 000 is forbidden. Reading the table as: cell(row=src, col=dst) = input symbol. Option B correctly places the only dead-state transition (00,0)->q and all other transitions consistent with the shift-register rule.

solution.result: B. The missing arcs are: from state 00 on 0 go to q (dead state), from state 00 on 1 go to 01; from state 01 on 1 go to 11; from state 10 on 1 go to 01 (or 11 depending on table reading); from state 11 on 0 go to 10; matching option B in the given tables.

questions.formula_note: Shift-register DFA construction: states encode the last k-1 symbols; transitions shift in the new symbol; a window of k consecutive symbols that violates the constraint (here, 000) triggers a transition to the dead/trap state q.
